When in Trip: Day 4 or 5 (already sleeping at Cathedral Valley Inn)
Name / Change: Temple of the Sun — Cathedral Valley
Why: A 400-foot isolated red sandstone monolith rising from a flat plain in Capitol Reef's remote north district. Almost no crowds. Arguably the most spectacular thing in the entire region that almost no tourists see.
Action / Notes: She is already sleeping 3 nights at Cathedral Valley Inn — she is literally at the doorstep. Access via Hartnet Road from Hwy 24 near Caineville. Dirt road — passable when dry. 4WD helpful but not required.

When in Trip: Day 4 or 5 (already sleeping at Cathedral Valley Inn)
Name / Change: Temple of the Moon — Cathedral Valley
Why: The companion formation to Temple of the Sun — visit both in the same morning. Same access road.
Action / Notes: Same access as Temple of the Sun. Both formations together take about 2 hours total including the drive in.

When in Trip: Day 4 or 5 (already sleeping at Cathedral Valley Inn)
Why: A massive sinkhole (100 ft wide 60 ft deep) in the middle of the flat valley floor. Very unusual and eerie geology. Located on the same loop road as the Temples.
Action / Notes: Same Hartnet Road loop as the Temples. All three (Temples + Sinkhole) can be done as a single 4-5 hour loop from the inn.

When in Trip: Day 19 (Zion — currently shows only Visitor Center)
Why: One of the most famous hikes in the US. Chain-assisted final section to a narrow summit ridge with full canyon views. Strenuous — 5.4 miles round trip.
Action / Notes: PERMIT REQUIRED — book immediately at recreation.gov (Zion National Park > Angels Landing Permit). Available by daily lottery. If permit unavailable: Observation Point is a comparable permit-free alternative (harder hike but better view).

When in Trip: Day 19 or 20 (Zion — currently shows only Visitor Center)
Why: Hike up the Virgin River through towering slot canyon walls — water up to waist in spring. Iconic and completely unique. No permit needed for the standard bottom-up day hike.
Action / Notes: Rent neoprene socks/wetsuit in Springdale BEFORE entering — May water is very cold (~45F). Multiple rental shops on Springdale main street. Take the shuttle to Temple of Sinawava (last stop). Budget 4-6 hours.

When in Trip: Day 20 (Zion — currently shows only Visitor Center)
Why: Three tiered pools with waterfalls through lush canyon vegetation. A beautiful lighter hiking option — ideal as a second Zion day or recovery hike.
Action / Notes: Lower Pool: Easy 0.6mi loop. Middle Pool: Moderate 1.0mi. Upper Pool: Moderate-Strenuous 1.2mi. Take shuttle to The Grotto stop. Great for photography.

Why: Short 1-mile trail to a sweeping viewpoint over Zion Canyon. Great warm-up hike on arrival. No shuttle needed.
Action / Notes: Trailhead at the east side of the Zion-Mt Carmel Tunnel on Hwy 9. Park in the pullout east of the tunnel. About 1 hour round trip.

Name / Change: Move hotel to Springdale instead of St. George for at least 1 night
Why: St. George hotel is 45+ minutes from the Zion shuttle stop. A Springdale hotel is 5 minutes away — enables early morning start for Angels Landing (must start by 7am) and full-day Narrows.
Action / Notes: Many options: Hampton Inn Springdale (37.199 -112.987) — easy to book. Zion Lodge (in the park) — books out far in advance. This is especially important if she gets the Angels Landing permit.

When in Trip: Between Grand Canyon and Page (Day 14-15 area)
Name / Change: Add one night at Monument Valley — The View Hotel
Why: The iconic Mittens photograph of Monument Valley REQUIRES golden hour light (sunrise or sunset). Arriving mid-day gives flat harsh light and a completely different experience.
Action / Notes: The View Hotel is run by the Navajo Nation and sits right on the canyon rim. Book far in advance. If she cannot stay overnight — at minimum plan arrival by 6pm for golden hour light.
